tickets.title: Functional identity transfer rescue test
acceptance: Extends the identity-thinning rescue benchmark with functional identity transfer/generalization probes that require applying the carried identity rule to novel starts and longer horizons after distractors.
Compares compressed_control, norm_floor_rescue, marker_info_rescue, and combined_rescue at activation_l1=0.125 while keeping sparsity pressure unchanged.
Uses the larger TAC-205 through TAC-209 local configuration with at least three seeds and 500 steps per variant/seed cell.

Reports identity retention, transfer accuracy, long-horizon transfer accuracy, identity-state magnitude, identity probe MI, energy feature MI, energy pair accuracy, rerank accuracy, LM accuracy, and compression score.
Determines whether norm-floor rescue improves functional identity transfer while marker-info rescue mainly improves passive probe-decodable identity information.
Writes JSON and Markdown artifacts under a new runs/benchmarks identity-transfer-rescue directory.
Updates research.md and progress.txt with whether TAC identity behaves more like dynamically usable state than a passive decodable marker.

Current result: extended experiments/benchmark_identity_thinning_rescue.py with novel-start and long-horizon identity transfer probes, then reran compressed_control, norm_floor_rescue, marker_info_rescue, and combined_rescue under runs/benchmarks/identity_transfer_rescue_tac211_2026_06_07. Norm-floor rescue still improves original retention from 0.3500 to 0.5500, but it does not improve functional transfer: transfer falls from 0.5333 to 0.4556 and long transfer falls from 0.5500 to 0.4389 relative to compressed_control. Marker-info rescue and combined rescue again produce high identity probe MI, 0.8994 and 0.9279, but do not produce strong transfer. Conclusion: TAC-209's causal rescue claim remains valid for identity retention, but TAC-211 does not support the stronger claim that current norm-floor or marker-information rescues restore generalized identity-rule transfer.

tickets.title: P8/P24 identity robustness causal benchmark
acceptance: Runs a post-hoc benchmark on the completed TAC-200 p8 and p24 5k checkpoints rather than on the newer p16/p24 runs.

Measures long-context identity carry by writing a target byte-pair identity fact in an earlier segment, omitting it from the active query segment, and scoring forced-choice next-byte identity retrieval.
Measures memory persistence and interference resistance across 0, 4, 8, and 16 intervening distractor identity pairs.
Measures active-context compression by varying the active query token budget from 4 to 48 tokens while carrying identity state.

Extends program knockout evidence beyond LM loss deltas by measuring identity retrieval accuracy drop, retrieval margin drop, memory-read margin drop, and program-memory norm drop for every p8 and p24 program.
Writes JSON and Markdown artifacts under runs/benchmarks/p8_p24_identity_robustness_2026_06_07.

Current result: decision.status=no_reliable_identity_carry_advantage_detected. p8 carry retrieval accuracy is 0.1667 versus reset 0.1875; p24 carry retrieval accuracy is 0.1875 versus reset 0.2083. Neither model reaches 50% retrieval accuracy at any tested active-context budget. Program knockouts produce no positive retrieval-accuracy drop for either p8 or p24. Memory-read forced-choice accuracy is higher at 0.5000 for p8 and 0.5208 for p24, but margins remain negative and do not produce downstream query retrieval. This does not support the claim that p24's higher specialization MI is causally useful for identity retention, interference resistance, or active-context compression in the first trained p8/p24 set.

tickets.title: Forced identity-state objective causality test
acceptance: Tests the TAC-212 diagnosis that specialization emerges but is not rewarded as a causal state carrier by the current objective.
Compares a context-visible LM objective against a forced-state objective where support key/value pairs are processed in a prefill segment and the query segment contains only QUERY,key.

Measures full-context accuracy, carried-state query accuracy, reset-state query accuracy, shuffled-state query accuracy, direct memory-read accuracy, active-context compression, program knockout retrieval drops, and gradient share through identity/program parameters.
Runs at least three seeds locally on a small TAC configuration before updating the research status.
Writes JSON and Markdown artifacts under runs/benchmarks/forced_identity_objective_tac213_2026_06_07.

Current result: decision.status=forced_identity_objective_not_sufficient. The context-visible objective reaches 0.3359 full-context accuracy but has carry=reset=0.0794 when support is removed from active context. The forced-state objective has carry=reset=0.0807, so it still does not learn usable carried-state query behavior. Direct memory-read accuracy is 0.3529 for both variants, showing that some support signal exists in the state, but it is not converted into query logits. Forced-state identity/program gradient share is nonzero but small at 0.0353, and max program-knockout retrieval drop is only 0.0104 with no harmful programs above 5 percentage points. Conclusion: simply making the query depend on carried state through the existing LM head is not sufficient; the next bottleneck is the bridge from identity memory/readout into answer logits and localized program causality.

tickets.title: Oracle identity-readout probe
acceptance: Freezes a forced-state TAC base trained with the TAC-213 task rather than changing the architecture.
Trains a tiny linear oracle probe from identity memory readout vector to value-token answer class.
Scores the probe as forced choice over the 12 value tokens and compares it with direct memory-read logits.
Runs three local seeds using the same 240-step forced-state base schedule as TAC-213.
Writes JSON and Markdown artifacts under runs/benchmarks/identity_readout_bridge_tac214_215_2026_06_07.

Current result: oracle_probe_accuracy=0.3190 and direct_memory_read_accuracy=0.3789. This is clearly above 12-way chance but far below a 90% oracle ceiling, so TAC-214 does not support the claim that identity readout already contains a nearly complete answer representation. The memory/readout signal is present but weak-to-moderate.

tickets.title: Direct identity-readout logit injection bridge
acceptance: Uses the same frozen forced-state TAC base and identity readout vectors as TAC-214.
Trains only a small linear bridge that adds readout-derived logits to the frozen query value-token logits.
Compares bridge carry accuracy against frozen base carry, reset bridge, zero-read bridge, and shuffled-read bridge controls.
Measures every-program bridge knockout accuracy drop to test localized program causality.
Runs three local seeds and records the bridge decision in the TAC-214/TAC-215 artifact.

Current result: decision.status=logit_bridge_partially_recovers_carried_state_answers. Base carry value accuracy is 0.1029 with carry-reset=0.0000; logit_bridge_accuracy rises to 0.3073, for a +0.2044 gain over base carry and +0.2174 over reset bridge. Shuffled readout falls to 0.1068, so the bridge is using carried readout information. Max bridge knockout drop is only 0.0130 and no program exceeds a 5 percentage-point harmful drop, so the bridge improves answer use without creating localized program causality.

tickets.title: Program-specific identity responsibility supervision
acceptance: Builds a key/value task where key KEY_START+p is assigned to program p, so target program responsibility is known.
Compares the original forced-state baseline, a semantic-routing baseline without assignment supervision, and a program-supervised semantic-routing variant.
For program-supervised training, applies support-token route supervision and target-program-slot value supervision while keeping the same forced-state answer objective.
After training, freezes the TAC model and trains post-hoc global readout bridge, target-slot probe, and target-slot bridge heads.
Measures target-slot versus wrong-slot bridge accuracy, route target argmax rate, route target selected rate, targeted program knockout drop, nontarget knockout drop, and localized drop gap.
Writes JSON and Markdown artifacts under runs/benchmarks/program_specific_supervision_tac216_2026_06_07.

Current result: decision.status=program_routes_align_but_slots_remain_noncausal. Program supervision raises route_target_selected_rate from 0.2678 in the semantic baseline to 0.9364, and route_target_argmax_rate from 0.1385 to 0.9390. It also improves target_slot_probe_accuracy from 0.1654 to 0.2617 and target_slot_bridge_accuracy from 0.1549 to 0.2643. However target_slot_bridge_minus_wrong_slot is only 0.0026, targeted_knockout_drop is -0.0022, localized_drop_gap is -0.0078, and no program has a target drop above 5 percentage points. Conclusion: explicit assignment can control routing and weakly improve slot decodability, but it does not make the assigned program slot causally necessary for the answer under this objective.

tickets.title: Representation binding and causal scrubbing fork
acceptance: Implements Direction A by comparing a semantic-routing baseline against a subspace-bound variant with fixed orthogonal program subspace masks.

Trains both variants on the TAC-216 key-to-program task and measures route target selected rate, outside-subspace ratio, global bridge accuracy, raw slot bridge accuracy, bound-slot probe accuracy, bound-slot bridge accuracy, wrong-bound-slot bridge accuracy, and targeted knockout drop.
Implements Direction B by scrubbing learned bridge rowspace, random subspace, top gradient dimensions, random dimensions, and attention output during feature extraction.
Runs three local seeds with 240 base steps, 200 head steps, binding diagnostics, targeted knockouts, and scrubbing diagnostics.
Writes JSON and Markdown artifacts under runs/benchmarks/representation_binding_scrubbing_tac217_2026_06_07.

Current result: decision.status=binding_and_scrubbing_not_sufficient. Direction A is not supported: subspace-bound routing reaches route_target_selected_rate=1.0000 but bound_slot_bridge_accuracy is only 0.0924, bound_slot_bridge_minus_wrong is 0.0052, and targeted_knockout_drop is -0.0043. Direction B is not supported: slot_subspace_projection_drop is -0.0065, random_subspace_projection_drop is 0.0065, gradient_scrub_drop is 0.0000, and attention_ablation_drop is only 0.0234. Conclusion: this first orthogonal subspace binding intervention over-constrains or fails to align useful identity/action signal; learned representation scrubbing of the weak bound-slot bridge does not reveal a stronger causal representation-level dependency.

tickets.title: Identity-conditioned decision continuity across time
acceptance: Carries prior TAC decision selections in IdentityState across forward calls.
Biases the next TAC program-decision surface toward carried prior decisions only when an identity state is supplied.
Reports a decision-continuity auxiliary loss and metric for carried-state temporal agreement.
Keeps fresh/reset TAC routing behavior unchanged when no prior identity state is supplied.
Adds focused regression coverage for identity-conditioned temporal decision continuity.

Current result: added IdentityState.decision_memory plus decision_continuity_strength, decision_continuity_decay, and decision_continuity_loss_weight controls to TACConfig. TAC routing now conditions energy, semantic, sparse-ensemble, hash, expert-choice, and authority-gated route decisions on carried decision memory when present; fresh/reset calls with no prior decision memory remain unconditioned. The model reports aux.losses['decision_continuity'], metric_decision_continuity_agreement, and metric_decision_continuity_memory_mass, and the trainer includes the loss in default auxiliary weighting. Focused regression proves carried decision memory can force the next route while an otherwise identical state without decision memory does not. Full TAC architecture tests pass with 182 tests.

tickets.title: BDH-inspired TAC adaptation probe
acceptance: Researches the Dragon Hatchling paper claims relevant to TAC: inference-time Hebbian working memory, sparse positive activations, modular/heavy-tailed graph structure, GPU-friendly state-space formulation, state memory, and interpretability-by-architecture.
Adds opt-in TACConfig.program_activation_type with sigmoid default plus relu and softplus positive activation modes.
Adds opt-in memory_write_type='hebbian_outer' so program memory can be updated as a gated outer product of routed program key state and candidate value state during inference.
Keeps existing TAC defaults unchanged.
Adds focused regression coverage for sparse positive activations, Hebbian selected-slot memory writes, and the BDH adaptation benchmark contract.
Adds experiments/benchmark_bdh_tac_adaptations.py and writes runs/benchmarks/bdh_tac_adaptations_2026_06_10/bdh_tac_adaptations.json.

Current result: decision.status=bdh_adaptations_locally_supported. Hebbian selected memory norm is 0.1542 with unselected norm 0.0 in the local probe; ReLU activation density is 0.5 versus sigmoid density 1.0; stateful MoE continued route agreement is 1.0 versus fresh 0.0; carried-vs-reset logits differ by 0.000925; selective-state mixer output is finite. Boundary: local structural probes only, not a trained external checkpoint improvement claim.

tickets.title: Memory-energy architecture research matrix
acceptance: Researches memory-agent and uncertainty mechanisms relevant to TAC while preserving TAC's objective: persistent computational identity for long-horizon agents.

Ranks mechanisms under the policy 'borrow mechanisms, not objectives': multi-timescale memory, procedural memory, memory consolidation, learned memory policies, retention valves, energy/unknown veto, state-space updates, and world-model integration.
Uses primary sources for the research basis: Memp, CMA, MemFactory, RATE, Recall-to-Imagine, EOW-Softmax, IDK-token uncertainty, and distributional energy-based verification.
Adds experiments/benchmark_memory_energy_architecture.py with deterministic local probes for layered memory consolidation and energy/uncertainty veto behavior.
Adds focused regression coverage in tests_py/test_memory_energy_architecture.py for priority ordering, causal carry-vs-reset behavior, noise retention, and hallucination-veto tradeoffs.
Writes runs/benchmarks/memory_energy_architecture_tac220_2026_06_10/memory_energy_architecture.json.

Current result: decision.status=promote_tac220_memory_energy_research. In the local simulation, layered memory improves task success from 0.70 to 1.00, reduces noise retention from 0.2083 to 0.0, and shows carry-reset delta 0.30. The energy/unknown veto reduces hallucination rate from 0.3889 to 0.0 and raises precision from 0.6111 to 1.0 at 0.6111 coverage. Boundary: deterministic simulation and research prioritization only, not a trained TAC checkpoint result. Superseded for evidence quality by TAC-221 actual model training, which did not validate the full mechanism claim.

tickets.title: Actual TAC multi-timescale memory and energy-veto validation
acceptance: Implements model-facing multi-timescale IdentityState fields: working_state, episodic_state, semantic_state, procedural_state, and memory_confidence.
Adds memory_system_type='multi_timescale' plus retention, consolidation, and procedural-memory rates to TACConfig without changing flat defaults.
Uses state-space-style retain/write updates for episodic, semantic, and procedural memory tiers.
Wires multi-timescale memory into compressed identity attention so tier ablations are real downstream interventions.
Runs actual TAC model training, not simulation, on a randomized support-query task where reset-state models cannot infer the answer from the query key alone.
Compares flat_control against multi_timescale across seeds 7, 19, and 31 with carry, reset, semantic/procedural ablation, unknown-answer, and veto metrics.
Writes runs/benchmarks/memory_energy_experimental_tac221_2026_06_10/memory_energy_experimental_validation.json.

Current result: decision.status=not_validated. Multi-timescale carry accuracy is 0.2410 versus flat carry 0.2016 and multi-timescale reset 0.0876, so carried state helps and multi-timescale gives a small lift. However semantic_procedural_ablation_accuracy remains 0.2410, identical to carry accuracy, so semantic/procedural tiers are not causally validated. Unknown accuracy remains 0.0; veto reduces accepted unknown hallucination rate from forced 1.0 to 0.1231 but only at 0.099 coverage. Conclusion: actual training does not yet support promoting procedural/semantic memory or the energy/unknown veto as solved TAC mechanisms.

tickets.title: Model-native semantic/procedural tier bridge validation
acceptance: Adds opt-in TACConfig.memory_bridge_type with none, multi_timescale_readout, and semantic_procedural_readout modes while preserving the default no-bridge behavior.
Adds TACConfig.memory_bridge_weight and trainable bridge projections that read carried memory-tier slots before the LM head.
Reports memory_bridge_update_norm and memory_bridge_tier_entropy metrics for bridge audits.
Updates parameter-count estimation for the bridge's trainable model-level parameters.
Adds focused regression coverage proving semantic/procedural tier state changes logits and receives gradient through the native LM loss path.

Runs actual TAC model training, not simulation, comparing multi_timescale_no_bridge against semantic_procedural_bridge across seeds 7, 19, and 31 with randomized values, carry, reset, and semantic/procedural ablation controls.
Writes runs/benchmarks/memory_tier_bridge_tac222_2026_06_10/memory_tier_bridge_validation.json.

Current result: decision.status=not_validated. The bridge creates a real aggregate semantic/procedural ablation drop of 0.0339, and bridge carry accuracy 0.2134 beats bridge reset 0.1454. However it only improves carry accuracy over the no-bridge control by 0.0051, far below the 0.05 validation threshold, and seed 31 collapses to 0.0946 carry accuracy. Unknown accuracy remains 0.0. Conclusion: a native semantic/procedural bridge makes the tiers causally visible, but this implementation is not a reliable performance improvement and does not solve calibrated unknown handling.

tickets.title: Stateful MoE agentic decision validation
acceptance: Tests the decomposition requested by the user: stateless MoE vs TAC stateful MoE vs TAC stateful MoE plus Hebbian memory.
Uses actual TACTransformerLM training, not deterministic simulation.
Uses an observe -> plan -> act/feedback -> verify task with randomized per-episode actions so future verification cannot be solved from the key alone.
Measures verify accuracy, repair-specific verify accuracy, reset-state verify accuracy, shuffled-state verify accuracy, route entropy, active programs, and program-memory mass.
Uses three seeds, reset and shuffled-state interventions, and a no-cross-call-state stateless MoE control.
Writes runs/benchmarks/stateful_moe_agentic_tac223_2026_06_11/stateful_moe_agentic_validation.json.

Current result: decision.status=not_validated. Stateless MoE verify accuracy is 0.1354, TAC stateful MoE verify accuracy is 0.1250, and TAC stateful MoE plus Hebbian verify accuracy is 0.1250 after 60 local training steps. Stateful reset and shuffled controls both equal carry at 0.1250, so there is no causal state advantage. Hebbian memory lowers memory mass from 0.2120 to 0.1226 without improving decisions. Conclusion: this first actual agentic decision benchmark does not support a claim that current persistent identity state, stateful routing, or Hebbian writes improve delayed repair/verify decisions under this training setup.

tickets.title: Verifier energy agentic validation
acceptance: Tests the user's requested hallucination/verification path with actual TAC training, not simulation.
Compares a stateful_control answer-loss TAC against verifier_energy, the same TAC wrapped with a trainable contrastive candidate energy head.
Uses observe -> plan -> feedback -> verify episodes with randomized actions, repair cases, and unsupported verification queries requiring UNKNOWN.

Measures verify accuracy, repair accuracy, unknown accuracy, reset and shuffled-state controls, energy-selected accuracy, energy pair-ranking accuracy, energy coverage, accepted accuracy, and accepted unsupported hallucination rate.
Measures causal interventions with carried state-slot knockout and low-rank expert-parameter knockout.
Uses three seeds and writes runs/benchmarks/verifier_energy_agentic_tac224_2026_06_11/verifier_energy_agentic_validation.json.

Current result: decision.status=not_validated. Verifier-energy and control have identical verify accuracy at 0.0972, identical repair verify accuracy at 0.1389, and identical state_advantage=0.0. The energy head improves pair ranking from 0.6250 to 0.7500 and energy-selected accuracy from 0.3056 to 0.3611, and reduces energy-selected unsupported hallucination from 0.6667 to 0.2333. However verifier energy coverage collapses to 0.0 at the fixed margin threshold, so accepted accuracy and accepted hallucination metrics do not validate useful selective answering. State-slot and expert-parameter knockout drops are both 0.0. Conclusion: contrastive energy supervision learns a weak candidate-ranking signal but does not yet improve TAC delayed decisions, causal state use, program causality, or calibrated answer acceptance.

tickets.title: Process-supervised agentic routing validation
acceptance: Tests whether explicit process-route supervision can bind TAC programs to memory-writer, planner, repair, verifier, and unknown roles.
Uses actual TACTransformerLM training, not simulation.
Compares stateful_control answer-only training against process_supervised answer plus route-role supervision.
Uses observe -> plan -> feedback -> verify episodes with randomized repair actions and unsupported verification queries requiring UNKNOWN.

Measures verify accuracy, repair accuracy, unknown accuracy, reset and shuffled-state controls, route-role accuracy, verifier-route accuracy, unknown-route accuracy, route entropy, active programs, state-slot knockout, and expert-parameter knockout.
Uses three seeds and writes runs/benchmarks/process_supervised_agentic_tac225_2026_06_11/process_supervised_agentic_validation.json.

Current result: decision.status=not_validated. Process supervision does not improve route-role accuracy: process_supervised and stateful_control are both 0.2526, with verifier_route_accuracy 0.2769 and unknown_route_accuracy 0.2393. Verify accuracy is identical at 0.1875, state_advantage remains 0.0, and shuffled/reset controls equal carry. Repair verify accuracy falls from 0.3444 in control to 0.3167 in process_supervised. State-slot knockout drop remains 0.0; expert-parameter knockout drop is 0.0 for process_supervised versus 0.0417 for control. Conclusion: this first route-role process objective does not bind programs into causal planner/repair/verifier/unknown specialists.

tickets.title: Hidden-state identifiability validation
acceptance: Researches LeJEPA's relevance to TAC as latent-state grounding rather than another memory mechanism.
Creates a hidden-rule task where TAC observes examples generated by an unobserved rule and must answer future transitions without receiving the rule label.
Uses actual TACTransformerLM answer training; hidden rule labels are reserved for post-hoc probes and evaluation.
Trains frozen linear probes from IdentityState to hidden rule and from IdentityState plus query cue to future transition.

Measures carry, reset, shuffled-state, hidden-rule probe accuracy, future-transition probe accuracy, same-rule/different-rule state cosine, observation-invariance gap, state-slot knockout, and expert-parameter knockout.
Uses three seeds and writes runs/benchmarks/hidden_state_identifiability_tac226_2026_06_11/hidden_state_identifiability_validation.json.

Current result: decision.status=not_validated. Carry accuracy is 0.2778, reset accuracy is 0.1944, and shuffled accuracy is also 0.2778, so carry does not beat shuffled state. Hidden-rule probe accuracy is 0.2500, chance for four rules. Future-transition probe accuracy is 0.2500, near chance for six transition classes under the short bounded run. Same-rule state cosine is 0.9479 and different-rule state cosine is 0.9476, leaving an observation-invariance gap of only 0.0003. State-slot and expert-parameter knockout drops are both 0.0. Conclusion: current IdentityState is not grounded as an identifiable hidden task state under this LeJEPA-inspired benchmark.

tickets.title: IdentityState bottleneck readout validation
acceptance: Tests the user's fused diagnosis by forcing final answers through IdentityState rather than the token hidden-state LM head.
Uses actual TACTransformerLM training wrapped with state-only hidden-rule and future-transition heads.
Trains hidden-rule and transition objectives directly from IdentityState features plus query cue, with a geometry regularizer separating different latent rules.

Measures hidden-rule accuracy, future-transition/carry accuracy, reset accuracy, shuffled accuracy, same-rule/different-rule state cosine, observation-invariance gap, state-slot knockout, and expert-parameter knockout.
Uses three seeds and writes runs/benchmarks/state_bottleneck_readout_tac227_2026_06_11/state_bottleneck_readout_validation.json.

Current result: decision.status=not_validated. The hard state-only path improves state geometry relative to TAC-226: same-rule cosine is 0.9038, different-rule cosine is 0.8146, and the observation-invariance gap rises to 0.0893. However hidden-rule accuracy is only 0.3958, future-transition/carry accuracy is 0.1736, reset accuracy is 0.2361, and shuffled accuracy is 0.1597. State advantage is negative at -0.0625, state-slot knockout drop is only 0.0556, and expert-parameter knockout drop remains 0.0. Conclusion: forcing a state readout starts to separate latent-rule geometry, but it still does not create reliable hidden-state recovery, state-causal decisions, or expert causality.

tickets.title: State pretraining before action training
acceptance: Tests whether IdentityState can first become an identifiable latent task state before being used for decisions.

Uses a two-stage actual TAC training protocol: Stage 1 trains state encoder, hidden-rule head, and future-transition head; Stage 2 freezes the state encoder/projector and trains a separate action head.
Keeps visible token hidden states out of the Stage 2 answer head, which reads only IdentityState features plus query cue.
Measures Stage 1 hidden-rule accuracy, Stage 1 future-transition accuracy, Stage 2 carry/reset/shuffled accuracy, state geometry, state-slot knockout, and expert-parameter knockout.
Uses three seeds and writes runs/benchmarks/state_pretraining_before_action_tac228_2026_06_11/state_pretraining_before_action_validation.json.

Current result: decision.status=not_validated with decision.failure_mode=transition_grounding_failed. Stage 1 hidden-rule accuracy reaches 0.9167 and state geometry is strongly structured: same-rule cosine is 0.9588, different-rule cosine is 0.1702, and observation-invariance gap is 0.7886. However Stage 1 future-transition accuracy is only 0.2708. With the state encoder frozen, Stage 2 carry accuracy is 0.2847, reset accuracy is 0.2639, shuffled accuracy is 0.2986, state-slot knockout drop is 0.0556, and expert-parameter knockout drop remains 0.0. Conclusion: two-stage pretraining can make IdentityState identify the hidden rule, but it does not yet ground that state into future-transition or action prediction.

tickets.title: State-query binding validation
acceptance: Tests the narrowed TAC-228 diagnosis: whether a rule-identifiable IdentityState can compose with a query cue to predict the correct transition.
Uses actual TACTransformerLM state encoder training, not simulation, followed by frozen-state explicit binding heads.
Pretrains the state encoder only for hidden-rule identifiability, then freezes it before transition binding training.
Compares a concat/product binding head against a bilinear binding head over IdentityState embedding plus query cue.
Measures hidden-rule accuracy, future-transition/carry accuracy, reset accuracy, shuffled accuracy, state advantage, state geometry, state-slot knockout, and expert-parameter knockout reported only.
Uses three seeds and writes runs/benchmarks/state_query_binding_tac229_2026_06_12/state_query_binding_validation.json.

Current result: decision.status=validated. Both concat/product and bilinear binding heads reach hidden-rule accuracy 1.0000, future-transition accuracy 1.0000, and carry accuracy 1.0000. Reset accuracy is 0.2315 and shuffled accuracy is 0.2477, keeping controls near chance while state advantage is 0.7685. State geometry remains strongly structured with same-rule cosine 0.9511, different-rule cosine 0.0581, and observation-invariance gap 0.8930. State-slot knockout drops are 0.3472 for concat/product and 0.3611 for bilinear. Expert-parameter knockout drop is 0.0 and is intentionally ignored for this ticket because experts were excluded from the validation gate. Conclusion: after rule-identifiable state pretraining, an explicit state-query binding module can ground IdentityState into transition/action prediction under this controlled benchmark.

tickets.title: Expert-causal state-query binding
acceptance: Tests whether specialist programs can become causally necessary once TAC-229 state-query binding is working.
Keeps the TAC-229 hidden-rule task and actual TACTransformerLM state encoder training.
Freezes the rule-identifiable state encoder before training a route-supervised expert binding head.
Assigns one transition family to each expert and trains route-role supervision so the state-derived route selects the matching expert.

Measures hidden-rule accuracy, carry accuracy, reset accuracy, shuffled accuracy, state-slot knockout, correct expert knockout, wrong expert knockout, expert knockout selectivity gap, and route-role accuracy.
Defines near-chance controls explicitly as four-rule chance 0.25 plus tolerance 0.05, so reset and shuffled controls must be <= 0.30.
Uses three seeds and writes runs/benchmarks/expert_causal_state_query_binding_tac230_2026_06_12/expert_causal_state_query_binding.json.

Current result: decision.status=validated. Hidden-rule accuracy is 1.0000, future-transition/carry accuracy is 1.0000, reset accuracy is 0.2693, shuffled accuracy is 0.2407, and state advantage is 0.7307. Route-role accuracy is 1.0000. Correct expert knockout drop is 1.0000, wrong expert knockout drop is 0.0, and expert knockout selectivity gap is 1.0000. State-slot knockout drop is 0.3426. State geometry remains strong with same-rule cosine 0.9511, different-rule cosine 0.0581, and observation-invariance gap 0.8930. Conclusion: with explicit route-role supervision and one expert per transition family, specialist experts become causally necessary on top of the validated TAC-229 state-query binding path.

tickets.title: Native low-rank program causal binding
acceptance: Tests whether native TACTransformerLM low-rank program expert parameters can replace the explicit expert matrices used in TAC-230.
Keeps the TAC-229/TAC-230 hidden-rule task, actual TAC state encoder training, and frozen rule-identifiable IdentityState encoder.
Routes query cues through TAC native IdentityFieldLayer.program_expert_down, program_expert_up, and program_expert_bias parameters.
Uses route-role supervision so the state-derived route selects the native program matching the hidden rule.

Measures carry, reset, shuffled controls, route-role accuracy, state-slot knockout, correct native program parameter knockout, wrong native program parameter knockout, and program knockout selectivity gap.
Defines near-chance controls explicitly as four-rule chance 0.25 plus tolerance 0.05, so reset and shuffled controls must be <= 0.30.
Uses three seeds and writes runs/benchmarks/native_program_causal_binding_tac231_2026_06_12/native_program_causal_binding.json.

Current result: decision.status=validated. Hidden-rule accuracy is 1.0000, future-transition/carry accuracy is 1.0000, reset accuracy is 0.2346, shuffled accuracy is 0.2438, and state advantage is 0.7654. Route-role accuracy is 1.0000. Correct native program parameter knockout drop is 0.8657, wrong native program parameter knockout drop is 0.0, and program knockout selectivity gap is 0.8657. State-slot knockout drop is 0.3796. State geometry remains strong with same-rule cosine 0.9511, different-rule cosine 0.0581, and observation-invariance gap 0.8930. Conclusion: under this controlled state-query binding setup, TAC demonstrates causal state, causal state-query binding, and causal specialist computation through native low-rank program parameters.

tickets.title: Internal-route native program binding
acceptance: Tests whether TAC internal IdentityFieldLayer routing can replace the explicit state-derived route head used in TAC-231.
Keeps the TAC-229/TAC-231 hidden-rule task, actual TAC state encoder training, frozen rule-identifiable IdentityState encoder, and native low-rank program parameters.
Forms a state-query hidden input, then lets IdentityFieldLayer.forward produce selected_program_mask and program_context through TAC's internal routing surface.
Uses native IdentityFieldLayer.program_expert_down, program_expert_up, and program_expert_bias for specialist computation.

Measures hidden-rule accuracy, carry accuracy, reset accuracy, shuffled accuracy, internal route-role accuracy from selected_program_mask, state-slot knockout, correct native program parameter knockout, wrong native program parameter knockout, and selectivity gap.
Defines near-chance controls explicitly as four-rule chance 0.25 plus tolerance 0.05, so reset and shuffled controls must be <= 0.30.
Uses three seeds and writes runs/benchmarks/internal_route_native_program_binding_tac232_2026_06_12/internal_route_native_program_binding.json.

Current result: decision.status=validated. Hidden-rule accuracy is 1.0000, future-transition/carry accuracy is 0.9915, reset accuracy is 0.2508, shuffled accuracy is 0.2508, and state advantage is 0.7407. Internal route-role accuracy is 0.9167. Correct native program parameter knockout drop is 0.7778, wrong native program parameter knockout drop is 0.0069, and program knockout selectivity gap is 0.7708. State-slot knockout drop is 0.3241. State geometry remains strong with same-rule cosine 0.9511, different-rule cosine 0.0581, and observation-invariance gap 0.8930. Seed-level caveat: seed 19 route-role accuracy is 0.75 and seed 7 state-slot knockout is 0.25, so this is an aggregate validation with some variance. Conclusion: TAC can use internal identity-field routing to select native causal programs under the controlled state-query hidden-input benchmark.

tickets.title: Near-native LM-head binding
acceptance: Tests whether TAC-232's state-query hidden adapter and custom action readout can be relaxed toward the ordinary TACTransformerLM query-token and lm_head answer path.
Keeps the hidden-rule task, actual TAC state encoder training, carried IdentityState, internal IdentityFieldLayer routing, and native low-rank program parameters.
Feeds the query as a normal token through TACTransformerLM.forward with carried identity_states and trains the normal lm_head to predict answer token Y_START + transition.

Measures candidate transition accuracy, full-vocabulary answer accuracy, reset and shuffled controls, internal route-role accuracy, state-slot knockout, correct native program parameter knockout, wrong native program parameter knockout, and program knockout selectivity gap.
Defines near-chance controls explicitly as four-rule chance 0.25 plus tolerance 0.05, so reset and shuffled controls must be <= 0.30.
Uses three seeds and writes runs/benchmarks/near_native_lm_head_binding_tac233_2026_06_12/near_native_lm_head_binding.json.

Current result: decision.status=not_validated. Hidden-rule accuracy remains 1.0000, but future-transition/carry accuracy falls to 0.2431 and full-vocabulary answer accuracy is also 0.2431. Reset accuracy is 0.2423 and shuffled accuracy is 0.2431, leaving state advantage at only 0.0008. Internal route-role accuracy collapses to 0.0177. Correct native program parameter knockout drop is 0.0, wrong native program parameter knockout drop is 0.0, program knockout selectivity gap is 0.0, and state-slot knockout drop is only 0.0278. Conclusion: the ordinary LM-head query path does not preserve TAC-232's causal routing/program mechanism under this objective; the remaining adapter/readout scaffold is still doing essential binding work.

tickets.title: Native state-query fusion variant sweep
acceptance: Tests the user's proposed fixes after TAC-233: residual fusion, input bottleneck, cross-attention bridge, process-supervised control, routing-token-style fusion, and activation steering.
Uses actual TAC training with the hidden-rule task, carried IdentityState, internal IdentityFieldLayer routing, native low-rank program parameters, and normal lm_head answer logits.
Runs a one-seed full screen across all six variants, then a focused three-seed validation on the two strongest contenders: input_bottleneck and residual_fusion.

Measures carry, reset, shuffled controls, full-vocabulary answer accuracy, internal route-role accuracy, state-slot knockout, correct native program parameter knockout, wrong native program parameter knockout, and selectivity gap.
Uses the same near-chance threshold as TAC-232/TAC-233: four-rule chance 0.25 plus tolerance 0.05, so reset and shuffled controls must be <= 0.30.
Writes final artifact runs/benchmarks/native_state_query_fusion_variants_tac234_2026_06_12/native_state_query_fusion_variants.json.

Current result: decision.status=not_validated. The focused three-seed run selects input_bottleneck as best by answer accuracy: hidden-rule accuracy 1.0000, carry/full-vocab accuracy 0.9931, reset 0.2662, shuffled 0.2269, state advantage 0.7269, and state-slot knockout 0.3056. However internal route-role accuracy is only 0.3912, correct program knockout drop is only 0.0116, wrong knockout is -0.0046, and selectivity gap is only 0.0162, so it solves the task by bypassing native causal programs. Residual_fusion is more mechanism-aligned but still fails: carry 0.7824, state advantage 0.4931, route-role 0.3866, correct program knockout 0.0810, wrong knockout 0.0440, selectivity gap 0.0370, and state-slot knockout 0.1944. Conclusion: simple native fusion bridges improve answer accuracy, but none yet preserve TAC-232's internal route/program causality under the normal lm_head path.

tickets.title: Native program bottleneck with anti-bypass loss
acceptance: Tests the TAC-234 diagnosis that simple fusion is insufficient because the LM path can still bypass selected native program output.

Compares anti-bypass variants: program_only_bottleneck, residual_hidden_dropout, gated_program_residual, input_program_bottleneck, state_query_program_bottleneck, and slot_conditioned_program_bottleneck.
Uses normal lm_head logits while forcing prediction through selected native low-rank program output and adding true-program answer, selected-route, and anti-bypass losses.
Runs a one-seed screen to identify promising bottlenecks, then a focused three-seed run on input_program_bottleneck and slot_conditioned_program_bottleneck.

Measures carry/full-vocabulary accuracy, reset/shuffled controls, state advantage, internal route-role accuracy, correct native program parameter knockout, wrong native program parameter knockout, program knockout selectivity gap, and state-slot knockout.
Uses near-chance threshold 0.30 for reset and shuffled controls.
Writes final artifact runs/benchmarks/native_program_bottleneck_antibypass_tac235_2026_06_12/native_program_bottleneck_antibypass.json.

Current result: decision.status=validated. Best variant is slot_conditioned_program_bottleneck. It reaches hidden-rule accuracy 1.0000, carry accuracy 0.9329, full-vocabulary answer accuracy 0.9306, reset accuracy 0.2014, shuffled accuracy 0.2477, state advantage 0.7315, internal route-role accuracy 0.8843, correct native program knockout drop 0.5718, wrong native program knockout drop 0.0046, program knockout selectivity gap 0.5671, and state-slot knockout drop 0.3519. Caveat: seed 19 route-role accuracy is 0.7431, so the aggregate validates but per-seed route stability is not perfect. Conclusion: making selected native program output a slot-conditioned bottleneck restores TAC-232-style program causality while answering through normal lm_head logits.

Copies a real multi-file repository slice into disposable sandbox workspaces and never edits the live repository.
Injects bugs that affect multiple copied files and runs failing tests before repair.
Applies localized multi-file patches only; full-file known-good restoration is forbidden and explicitly reported as full_file_restore_rate.

Runs tests after repair and reports real slice copy, multi-file bug injection, pre-patch test success, localized patch application, full-file restore rate, post-patch test success, test improvement, failure localization, responsible program selection, multi-file patch correctness, regression avoidance, sandbox repair success, and no-restore repair score.
Validates only when multi-file localized repairs improve failing tests without regressions and with near-zero full-file restoration.
Writes runs/benchmarks/tac270_multifile_sandbox_repair_no_restore/tac270_multifile_sandbox_repair_no_restore.json by default and supports --smoke.

tickets.title: Ambiguous multi-file repair stress
acceptance: Adds experiments/benchmark_tac271_ambiguous_multifile_repair_stress.py with run_tac271_ambiguous_multifile_repair_stress returning schema, method, repository_profile, sandbox_artifacts, per_seed, metrics, decision, and artifact_path.
Copies a real multi-file repository slice into disposable sandbox workspaces and never edits the live repository.
Injects ambiguous failures with incomplete tests, deceptive tests, conflicting repair objectives, and delayed verification by default.
Provides multiple plausible candidate repairs for each failure and measures whether the correct causal repair is selected before relying on retry.
Runs public and hidden/regression tests so a surface repair can pass public checks while still failing full verification.

Reports ambiguous failure copy, ambiguous bug injection, pre-patch test success, candidate fix count, plausible-fix disambiguation, incomplete-test guard, deceptive-test resistance, first-attempt failure, retry repair success, post-patch test success, test improvement, regression avoidance, ambiguity repair success, and ambiguity stress score.
Validates only when TAC handles ambiguity, resists incomplete/deceptive tests, recovers from wrong first attempts, and still improves full verification without regressions.
Writes runs/benchmarks/tac271_ambiguous_multifile_repair_stress/tac271_ambiguous_multifile_repair_stress.json by default and supports --smoke.

tickets.title: Causal fix disambiguation
acceptance: Adds experiments/benchmark_tac272_causal_fix_disambiguation.py with run_tac272_causal_fix_disambiguation returning schema, method, repository_profile, candidate_artifacts, per_seed, metrics, decision, and artifact_path.
Builds on TAC-271 ambiguous multi-file failures and scores multiple plausible candidate repairs before applying a patch.
Scores causal consistency, minimal edit distance, test coverage explanation, cross-file dependency impact, historical state consistency, responsible-program confidence, and predicted regression risk.
Applies the highest-scoring candidate first, then runs full verification and retry repair only if the first pass fails.

Reports candidate fix count, scoring feature aggregates, causal explanation alignment, first-pass disambiguation accuracy, post-patch test success, retry repair success, regression avoidance, and causal fix score.

Validates only when first-pass disambiguation is at least 0.65, post-patch success is at least 0.85, retry success is at least 0.90, regression avoidance is at least 0.90, and causal explanation alignment is at least 0.70.
Writes runs/benchmarks/tac272_causal_fix_disambiguation/tac272_causal_fix_disambiguation.json by default and supports --smoke.

tickets.title: Multi-bug long repair chain
acceptance: Adds experiments/benchmark_tac273_multi_bug_long_repair_chain.py with run_tac273_multi_bug_long_repair_chain returning schema, method, per_seed, metrics, decision, and artifact_path.
Tests whether TAC can handle multiple interacting bugs across several repair steps without state collapse.
Evaluates metric_artifact_contract, routing_state_regression, and compression_repair_interaction bug sets by default.
Sweeps repair chain lengths 3, 5, and 8 by default.
Reports first_pass_root_cause_set, chain_completion, regression_avoidance, average_repair_steps, repair_step_threshold, state_continuity, multi_bug_interaction_score, and repair_chain_score.

Validates only when first_pass_root_cause_set is at least 0.65, chain_completion is at least 0.70, regression_avoidance is at least 0.90, average_repair_steps is below the configured threshold, and state_continuity is at least 0.70.
Writes runs/benchmarks/tac273_multi_bug_long_repair_chain/tac273_multi_bug_long_repair_chain.json by default and supports --smoke.

tickets.title: Interaction-aware repair planning
acceptance: Adds experiments/benchmark_tac274_interaction_aware_repair_planning.py with run_tac274_interaction_aware_repair_planning returning schema, method, per_seed, metrics, decision, and artifact_path.
Builds a dependency graph between detected bugs before applying fixes.
Predicts patch order before applying repairs and tracks whether each patch creates, exposes, or resolves another failure.
Penalizes premature local fixes that block later chain completion.
Compares against the TAC-273 chain-completion baseline.

Reports dependency_graph_accuracy, patch_order_accuracy, interaction_tracking_accuracy, premature_fix_avoidance, root_cause_set, chain_completion, regression_avoidance, state_continuity, average_repair_steps, improvement_over_tac273, and interaction_aware_repair_score.

Validates only when chain_completion is at least 0.70, regression_avoidance is at least 0.90, state_continuity is at least 0.70, root_cause_set is at least 0.65, and improvement_over_TAC273 is at least +0.05.
Writes runs/benchmarks/tac274_interaction_aware_repair_planning/tac274_interaction_aware_repair_planning.json by default and supports --smoke.
